topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent lights of my cramped dorm room suddenly seeming too bright, too harsh. My hands, which had been trembling slightly as I clicked the link, clenched into fists so tight my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. "Denied." The single word echoed in the silence, a hollow, mocking sound. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound sharp and final. I stood up, pacing the small space between my desk and the window, my breath coming in short, ragged gasps. Every carefully crafted paragraph, every late night spent perfecting that essay, felt utterly worthless now. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling a hot flush creep up my neck. I wanted to scream at the screen, to physically shatter the digital evidence of this rejection.

[story 2]

I reread the rejection letter three times. Each time, a fresh wave of cold fury washed over me, settling deep in my stomach like ice water. My jaw was tight, muscles aching with the effort of keeping my expression neutral, though I could feel the tremor running through my entire body. I walked to my closet, pulling out my acceptance letter from last year, holding it up to the light as if expecting it to somehow magically transform into the acceptance I was owed. "This is absurd," I muttered, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I started pacing the length of my room, my footsteps heavy and deliberate on the worn carpet. I stopped abruptly by my bookshelf, gripping the spine of a thick textbook until my knuckles were white. The injustice of it all felt monumental, a heavy weight pressing down on my chest until breathing became a conscious, shallow effort.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I didn't move. I just sat there, frozen, my gaze locked onto the screen until the text blurred. A slow, deep burn started in my chest, spreading outwards until my whole frame felt taut and vibrating with suppressed energy. I slowly leaned back in my chair, the squeak of the worn material sounding deafening in the quiet room. I picked up my phone, intending to call someone, but my fingers hovered over the dial pad, paralyzed by a surge of sheer disbelief and indignation. I threw the phone onto the bedspread, the impact surprisingly muted. I stood up, moving with a jerky, agitated energy, circling the desk like a caged animal. I kicked lightly at the leg of my chair, the frustration manifesting as restless, uncontrolled movement.
